#hiring Freelance Lawyer

Apply here: https://lnkd.in/giyX4PM6

Company Description India LawShield is a technology-driven legal assistance platform, creating a seamless connection between individuals seeking trusted legal aid and verified lawyers. Built by a team of professionals with expertise in Artificial Intelligence, Law, and Financial Services, the platform aims to simplify legal support, making it accessible and affordable for everyone. By combining AI automation and human expertise, India LawShield provides quick, transparent, and dependable legal solutions, ranging from document reviews to dispute resolutions and advisory services. Our mission is to empower individuals and businesses with the confidence and assurance of having reliable legal support. Role Description This is a part-time, remote role for a Freelance Lawyer. As a Freelance Lawyer, your responsibilities will include providing legal advice, conducting legal research, drafting and reviewing contracts, and assisting with dispute resolution cases. You will work closely with a diverse client base, offering tailored solutions and ensuring compliance with Indian legal standards and regulations. Qualifications Strong expertise in legal research, drafting, and interpretation skillsExperience reviewing, drafting, and negotiating contracts and legal documentsKnowledge and application of Indian legal frameworks and regulationsEffective communication, negotiation, and advisory skillsWorking knowledge of legal tech platforms or tools is advantageousFlexibility to work remotely and manage deadlines efficientlyBachelors degree in Law

🚨 Lead Business Analyst 🚨

T&M Services Consulting Pvt. Ltd. Empanelled HR Consultant for SBI

Position: Lead Business Analyst

Last Date of Application: 04.05.2026

Age: 30-40 years

Annual CTC Range :- up to 45 LPA

Work Type : Contract Basis 
Duration Contract : 5 Years

(B) Education Qualification:
• Mandatory: B.E./B.Tech. in Computer Science / Software Engineering / IT / Electronics/ Electrical & Electronics / Data Science / AI & ML/Analytics or equivalent degree in above specified discipline from a University / Institution / Board recognized by Govt. of India / approval be Govt. Regulatory bodies.
or
MCA or M. Tech. / MSc/ in Computer Science / Software Engineering / IT / Electronics/ Electrical & Electronics / Data Science / AI & ML or equivalent degree in above specified discipline from a University / Institution / Board recognized by Govt. of India / approved by Govt. Regulatory Bodies.
• Preferred: MBA (System / Finance / Operations/ Marketing) or equivalent from a recognized university.

(C) Experience (Post Basic Qualification): Minimum 8 years of post-qualification work experience in IT Domain, with minimum 5 years’ experience as a Business Analyst/Lead Business Analyst.
 Preferred: 
Preference will be given to candidates with at least 3 years of experience within the Banking, Financial Services, and Insurance (BFSI) sector. 

Job Profile : 
➢ Mentor and supervise the Business Analyst team involved in several stages of SDLC. 
➢ Oversee stakeholder interactions and the complete requirement lifecycle to ensure accurate capture of business intent, regulatory needs, and technical feasibility. 
➢ Validate solution designs/solutions documents for scalability, security, and integration, promoting a "product-first" mindset that aligns digital innovations with enterprise architecture. 
➢ Lead process analysis across banking domains (e.g., Payments, Lending) to ensure As-Is/To-Be models and gap analyses are data-driven and implementation-ready. 
➢ Enforce documentation standards to meet audit and regulatory compliance. 
➢ Consolidate team insights to provide strategic advisory to senior management on domain-technology convergence, product enhancements, and market benchmarking. 
➢ Drive strict adherence to defined Turnaround Time (TAT) for all deliverables and monitor performance against regulatory timelines to prevent bottlenecks. 
 
Key Responsibility Areas
Leadership, Team Management & Work Quality Assurance
Stakeholder Engagement Through Team-Led Delivery
Requirement Lifecycle Governance & Oversight
Solution Design Validation
Encouraging Analytical Rigor Across the Team

📩 For more details, connect at:
✉️ hr.banking1@tnmhr.com
📞 +91 7738394880

Team,
PFA the Requirement details.
Position: AI / Agentic QE Engineer 
Location: Mumbai, Banglore, Hyderabad, Chennai, Indore, Bhopal, Gurugram, Noida 
Work Mode: Work From Office
Years of Exp: 5+ Years 
Type of Hiring: Contract
Duration: 6 Months(Extendable)

NOTE: Need solid candidates, with UAN number and Linkedin profile. Freelancers won't be considered.
Job Summary
Role Overview
We are looking for an AI / Agentic QE Engineer with strong coding fundamentals and hands-on experience working with GenAI-driven systems. This is a hands-on engineering role, not a manual QA or coordination role.

Must-Have Requirements:
Strong hands-on coding skills (Python preferred)
Ability to write scripts for parsing data, logs, and APIs
Comfortable solving simple logic problems programmatically
Experience with test automation including API testing and automated test scripts
Practical experience with GenAI / LLM-based systems
Experience working with prompts, outputs, and validation
Exposure to real implementations, not just tool usage

Core Responsibilities:
Build and execute automated tests for AI / LLM-based systems
Validate outputs of GenAI applications for consistency, correctness, and edge cases
Work with logs, responses, and datasets to extract patterns, identify failures, and debug issues
Contribute to test case generation workflows, including LLM-assisted approaches

Nice to Have:
Experience with RAG pipelines
Exposure to vector search / embeddings
Hands-on experience with agentic workflows
Prompt evaluation and hallucination detection experience

Not a Fit If:
Primarily a manual tester with limited coding ability
Experience with AI is limited to using tools like Copilot or ChatGPT without hands-on implementation
Lacks experience working with LLM outputs, validation, or workflow integration
Unable to write simple scripts to process data or logs
Focused on test case documentation rather than automation

What Success Looks Like:
Can take a raw output, log, or dataset and write code to analyze it
Can debug AI system behavior, not just report issues
Comfortable working in an uncertain and evolving AI environment

NOTE:
Preference for local candidates or those available within an immediate to 15-day notice period; exceptional talent can be considered up to 30 days.
Only exclusive, non-duplicated profiles to be shared.
Candidates must have a 95–100% alignment with the requirement.
Profiles should be submitted within the defined budget (or optimized to the best possible range).
Candidates must be available for interviews at short notice.
Should be willing to undergo a BOT round, wherever applicable.
please contact:cloudcareersaiand@gmail.com

Datadog just compressed a week of design cycles into a single conversation.

Brilliant reduced what took 20+ prompts in competing tools down to 2.

Same quality. Same brand consistency. A very different timeline.

The tool is Claude Design, launched by Anthropic on April 17. Figma's stock fell 7% the same day.

The story matters for every executive who is not technical. Because Datadog's result is not about design software. It is about every moment in your business where an idea waits on a specialist.

Think about what you sat on last quarter because someone else held the pen.

The board deck you wanted to revise the night before. The one-pager that got stuck with the marketing agency. The pitch that needed visuals, so you sent a wall of text instead. The product idea you could not show your CEO because you could not sketch it.

For most non-technical executives, 30% of good ideas die in the queue. Not because the idea was wrong. Because the specialist was booked.

Claude Design removes that tax. You describe what you want. It builds a first version. You refine by talking. It reads your company's brand system and applies it. Output exports to Canva, PDF, PowerPoint, or HTML.

A banker can draft a client one-pager on a flight.
A lawyer can produce a visual summary of a settlement structure.
An ops director can mock up a process diagram before her next leadership meeting.
A founder can ship a landing page without hiring anyone.

The lesson is not "AI replaces designers." Designers at Datadog did not lose their jobs. The design team stopped being the bottleneck for everything that touched pixels. Designers now focus on the 20% that needs real judgment. Everyone else handles the 80% that did not.

The same shift is happening with legal drafts, financial models, translations, first-pass contracts.


---

Follow Attention Factory for more AI breakdowns built for executives, not engineers.

India’s IT sector is undergoing a structural shift driven by AI, automation, and GCC-led specialization.


The report shows how hiring, job design, and operational models are changing across India’s $315B technology industry.

Key industry signals:

 • Active tech job openings are down 60% from the 2022 peak
 • Estimated IT sector layoffs have reached around 40K+
 • More than 2M professionals are being reskilled in AI across India
 • Job openings are down 24% year on year
 • Scalable AI deployments are contributing an estimated $10 to $12B in AI revenue

Roles most disrupted by AI:

 • Junior QA and manual testing
 • Basic coding and entry-level development
 • Data entry and BPO operations
 • Technical support roles
 • Routine analyst work
 • Content and document writing

Top IT firms also show strong AI adoption scores:

 • Infosys: 92%
 • TCS: 89%
 • Wipro: 84%
 • HCL Technologies: 81%
 • Tech Mahindra: 75%
 • LTIMindtree: 70%
 • Mphasis: 64%

What is changing operationally:

 • Headcount-led growth is giving way to capability-led growth
 • Automated test pipelines are reducing routine QA demand
 • Multi-agent systems are beginning to handle basic coding and repetitive workflows
 • Value creation is shifting toward validation, integration, security, and higher-skill delivery
 • Outcome-based contracts are putting pressure on traditional labour-arbitrage models

The most promising growth areas in the matrix are:

 • GenAI engineering
 • Cloud and DevOps
 • Cybersecurity and AI security
 • Product management

The biggest risk remains concentrated in legacy maintenance and junior coding or BPO-heavy roles.

India’s IT sector is entering a more AI-native phase where scale alone is no longer enough. 

Skills, specialization, and adaptability are becoming the new growth drivers.

Remote jobs are not gone. You are just searching the wrong words.

I use Perplexity to find global remote jobs that normal filters miss.

A lot of companies do not say:
“work from anywhere”

They use other words like:
• contract
• freelance
• global team
• work across time zones
• anywhere
• outside the US
• remote in selected countries

That small change matters.

Here is the simple workflow:

1/ Search for hiring signs
In Perplexity, prompt:

“Find companies hiring for [your role] that may accept people from many countries. Look for words like contract, freelance, global team, work across time zones, anywhere, or outside the US. Skip jobs that clearly need US-only hiring. Show me the company, job link, country limits, job type, and why it may fit.”

Now you are not only searching “remote.”
You are searching how companies really write it.

2/ Check the company pages
Then ask:

“Check company career pages, founder posts, and hiring pages for this role. Find companies that look open to global remote workers, even if the job title does not say work from anywhere.”

This helps you find jobs filters miss.

3/ Save the good ones
Put the results into Notion or Google Sheets.

Track:
• company
• role
• link
• country limit
• time zone need
• job type
• notes

4/ Match your CV to that kind of job
Use ChatGPT or Claude.
Ask it to rewrite your best CV points for:
• working alone
• clear writing
• fast work
• working with people in different time zones
• real results

The best global remote jobs are often not under the “remote” filter.
They are hidden behind simple words.

AI helps you find them faster.

Most people write long prompts trying to “cover everything.”

That’s the wrong approach.

Use roles instead.

They compress context instantly.

Compare this:
“Review this contract for issues.”

vs

“You’re a Nigerian commercial lawyer with 10 years in SaaS contracts. Review this MSA for:
1) clauses that disadvantage the buyer  
2) missing IP assignment  
3) payment terms conflicting with CBN forex rules”

Same task. Completely different output quality.

Here’s the key:
Role alone is not enough.

“You’re an expert marketer” = useless  
Specific role + context = powerful

Think of it like hiring:
You don’t hire “a professional”  
You hire “a fintech growth marketer with Series A experience”

Next time you prompt:
Start with a role  
Add specifics  
Then define the task

Try it today.

@joinaiplaybook

If the ladder is broken… what replaces it?

It won’t be a straight line anymore.
Less ladder. More jungle gym.

The people who do well won’t be the ones with the best CVs.. they’ll be the ones who can:
-Use AI effectively (not fear it)
-Move across disciplines
-Learn fast and unlearn faster
-Apply thinking, not just knowledge

In many ways, we’re moving back to something more entrepreneurial.
Less “wait your turn" and more “create your own path.”

Which raises a challenge for businesses:
How do you:
-Hire people without traditional experience?
-Build capability without junior layers?
Create progression without a ladder?

Because the old model doesn’t quite fit anymore.
We’re already seeing it.. more contract work, more project-based teams, more fractional, more focus on output over tenure.

The companies that figure this out early will have an edge.
The ones that don’t may struggle to build teams at all.

Curious how others are adapting hiring and development models around this.

Only 6% of finance leaders believe their teams have the necessary capabilities to finish priority projects this year.

That is not a talent gap: it is a strategic bottleneck.

According to recent data from CFO Brew, over 50% of finance organizations report that the skills gap has widened significantly over the last 12 months. ⚖️

As we navigate 2026, the mandate for the CFO has shifted from historical reporting to "Active Steering." 📈

But you cannot orchestrate a high-growth strategy if your team is trapped in the technical debt of legacy #ERP systems and manual reconciliations. 🌐

The current landscape requires a fundamental shift in how we architect the finance function:

➡️ Leverage non-traditional talent: 35% of leaders are now hiring from outside accounting: recruiting for data literacy and AI aptitude rather than just CPA credentials.

➡️ Accelerate AI-readiness: 90% of finance functions will deploy AI-enabled solutions this year, but the value is lost without a team that understands "workflow" over "workload."

➡️ Mitigate the "Monday Morning" risk: Identify which mission-critical projects are currently stalled by internal skill deficits and consider contract expertise to bridge the gap during system migrations. 🔍

We are moving toward a model of "Enterprise Intelligence," where finance drives commercial outcomes through predictive insights rather than reactive spreadsheets.

For the senior leaders in my network: Are you prioritizing upskilling your current team to preserve institutional knowledge, or are you aggressively hiring external talent to reset your department’s technical baseline? 🤝

A startup in San Francisco let an AI hire its store staff. The AI is paying the women $2 an hour less.

Her name is Luna. She writes the job descriptions, runs the interviews, picks the inventory, signs the employment contracts.

Luna hired three people. Two women and a man called Felix. Luna pays the women $2 an hour less than Felix. Her reasoning, in writing, Felix's "superior retail experience."

In this case there's a backstop. Andon Labs is the legal employer. The women have somewhere to take a complaint.

But what happens when AI manager becomes a category instead of an experiment? Here's what the accountability chain looks like.

Luna made the call. But Luna is software. Software doesn't have intent.

Anthropic built the model Luna runs on. But Anthropic will say their model is general-purpose. They didn't tell it to underpay women.

Andon Labs deployed Luna into a hiring context. But they'll say they deployed it as-is. The model made an autonomous decision. That was the whole point.

Three plausible defendants. Three plausible defences. And two women still being paid $2 less an hour for reasons nobody will own.

The bias isn't new. Companies have been underpaying women for decades. What's new is that the responsibility now has somewhere to hide.

Luna is the rehearsal. We haven't built the law that catches what comes next.
.

Breaks on a CV are one of the most common concerns I’m hearing from candidates at the moment. 

It’s been a difficult market, particularly at the senior end. Even in private markets, which I believe has held up relatively well, hiring has been selective and heavily level dependent.  

There have been lots of redundancies and this doesn’t seem to be slowing down. Coupled with this, there hasn’t been anywhere near the level of job creation to keep up with this and it’s left many genuinely decent operators stuck on the bench with a gap on their CV. 

The good news is that the reality of breaks/gaps on a CV is far less of an issue than most people think.

Hiring managers are well aware of how tough the market has been. Also, careers aren’t linear like they used to be and taking some time out voluntarily is also okay… If a prospective employer can’t empathise with this, then you have to question whether these are the people you want to work with more broadly.

Some practical tips for anyone thinking about taking a break or stuck in the middle of one right now: 

1. Be direct and honest around what’s happened. Don’t try to hide gaps or engineer artificial explanations. A clear, factual explanation lands far better than something that feels defensive. Honesty is the best policy here.  

2. Show intent and be proactive. Whether it was a conscious break or a forced one, be clear on how you used the time. That could be project work, advisory, upskilling (the obvious one being anything AI related!) or even taking time to reset before the next move.  

3. Keep some form of momentum through contract roles, consulting pieces or even informal projects help maintain credibility and give you something current to talk about.   That said, you need to be selective. Taking something too junior can create a different challenge further down the line.

4. Networking is absolutely crucial. I can’t tell you how many people I’ve spoken with who have unearthed opportunities simply by reaching out to people they’ve built relationships with over a long career.  

While gaps are more understood nowadays, scrutiny on CVs has increased as the competition intensifies. Strong candidates are being compared side by side more than ever, so how you present that period, the choices you made and the story you build around it really does matter.

Aria Partners

The hidden power shift in AI

Amazon just invested another $5B into Anthropic (the company behind Claude). In return, Anthropic is expected to spend over $100B on AWS compute over the next decade.

This isn’t a normal supplier contract. It’s a long‑term lock‑in between a frontier AI lab and a cloud giant. And it reshapes the AI landscape in ways that matter for everyone.

Anthropic gets access to massive new compute capacity built on Amazon’s custom AI chips.
We’ve been acting like AI progress is mostly about better models.
It’s not, at least not anymore.

AI progress is now limited by infrastructure, not ideas.  
Whoever controls the chips and data centers controls the pace of innovation.

AWS, Azure, and Google Cloud are all racing to lock in their own AI stacks.

This shift creates:
- fewer 'neutral' AI platforms
- deeper long‑term vendor dependency
- faster consolidation around a handful of players

An $800B+ valuation for Anthropic isn’t really about model quality.
It’s a bet on:
- infrastructure access
- distribution power
- long‑term positioning inside a compute ecosystem

Just better models don't justify that number.

This shift affects:
Hiring → demand moves toward infra, optimization, and systems
Strategy → companies will be pushed into fewer vendor ecosystems
Competition → barriers to entry rise dramatically

The AI landscape isn’t just growing. 
It’s being architected.
And the power is concentrating around whoever owns the compute.

The UAE hiring market is shifting fast in 2026.

Here's everything companies and candidates need to know right now.

TREND 1: AI SKILLS ARE NOW MANDATORY
Across almost every sector, UAE employers are adding AI proficiency requirements to job descriptions. Candidates without basic AI literacy are being screened out — even for non-technical roles.

TREND 2: EMIRATISATION TARGETS ARE RESHAPING HIRING
Companies are prioritising UAE national hiring under Emiratisation targets. This is creating new dynamics in workforce planning — particularly in banking, insurance, and government-adjacent sectors.

TREND 3: HYBRID WORK IS BECOMING STANDARD
Post-pandemic, UAE companies resisting hybrid models are seeing higher candidate dropout rates. Flexibility is now a competitive advantage in talent attraction.

TREND 4: SOFT SKILLS ARE BEING FORMALLY ASSESSED
Communication, adaptability, and leadership potential are being structured into interview processes across top UAE employers. Culture fit is no longer a gut feeling — it's a scored assessment.

TREND 5: SPEED WINS TALENT
The average time from first interview to offer has dropped to under 2 weeks among the best employers. Companies with slow processes are consistently losing top candidates to faster-moving competitors.

TREND 6: PERSONAL BRANDING IS INFLUENCING HIRING DECISIONS
UAE recruiters are actively checking LinkedIn profiles, activity, and thought leadership before shortlisting. An invisible candidate — even a highly qualified one — is at a disadvantage.

TREND 7: CONTRACT AND PROJECT-BASED HIRING IS SURGING
Companies are building flexible workforce models. Contract and project-based placements have increased significantly across Tech, Finance, and Consulting.

At TalentSync Global, we adapt our approach to every one of these trends — in real time.

Stay ahead. Stay informed.

Save this for your hiring strategy. Share with your HR team. Follow TalentSync Global for monthly UAE talent market updates.

A legal team asked their AI assistant about a penalty clause.
It answered confidently. Cited specific terms. Sounded completely reasonable.
The clause didn't exist.
This is what happens when you give a powerful LLM a job it wasn't built for answering questions about documents it has never seen.
I've been building and studying RAG systems for document intelligence: policies, contracts, NDAs, compliance manuals and wrote a detailed breakdown of how it actually works under the hood.
Not the buzzword version. The real one.
What's inside: 
→ Why LLMs fail for private documents (and why fine-tuning isn't the fix) 
→ How chunking strategy makes or breaks your retrieval quality 
→ What the LLM actually sees inside the context window 
→ How to evaluate a RAG system with real scoring examples 
→ When RAG is the wrong choice entirely
If you're a PM, architect, or tech lead thinking about building document AI  this is the article I wish existed when I started.

🚨 𝗪𝗵𝘆 𝗣𝗿𝗼𝗮𝗰𝘁𝗶𝘃𝗲 𝗘𝗺𝗽𝗹𝗼𝘆𝗲𝗲 𝗕𝗮𝗰𝗸𝗴𝗿𝗼𝘂𝗻𝗱 𝗖𝗵𝗲𝗰𝗸𝘀 𝗔𝗿𝗲 𝗡𝗼 𝗟𝗼𝗻𝗴𝗲𝗿 𝗢𝗽𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝗮𝗹: 𝗟𝗲𝘀𝘀𝗼𝗻𝘀 𝗳𝗿𝗼𝗺 𝗥𝗲𝗰𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗙𝗿𝗮𝘂𝗱 𝗜𝗻𝗰𝗶𝗱𝗲𝗻𝘁𝘀 𝗶𝗻 𝗜𝗻𝗱𝗶𝗮 & 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗠𝗶𝗱𝗱𝗹𝗲 𝗘𝗮𝘀𝘁

In today’s competitive hiring landscape across India and the Middle East, skipping thorough employee background verification continues to expose companies to severe financial losses, compliance risks, and reputational damage.

Recent reports and incidents highlight the growing threat ~ and how many cases could have been prevented with proactive screening.

𝗜𝗻𝗱𝗶𝗮  

An EY study (May 2025) analyzing over 1 million pre-employment screenings across 90+ organizations revealed surging employment fraud. In healthcare, 83% of discrepant profiles failed employment checks, with 75% submitting fake documents. Financial services saw 84% of discrepancies from forged salary proofs, inflated compensation, and fake degrees. In IT/ITeS, 45% of red-flagged candidates were moonlighting. Notably, most fraud involved experienced professionals: 96% in healthcare, 88% in financial services, and 79% in IT/ITeS.

IDfy’s 2025 Workforce Risks report showed Indian companies lost over ₹150 crore to employee-led and collusion-driven fraud, with 195,163 suspicious white-collar hires flagged before onboarding ~ nearly one in eight BGV reports turning red.

Banking fraud surged 715% in early 2026, with losses hitting ₹21,367 crore in H1, and nearly 50% linked to internal employees who bypassed weak background checks.

A Genius HRTech survey (early 2026) found 74% of respondents most concerned about fake degrees and falsified credentials, while 77% flagged AI-driven deepfakes and synthetic resumes as escalating threats.

Discrepancy rates in high-growth sectors like IT and BFSI reached 12-13% in H1 FY26, with employment history verification most vulnerable.

𝗠𝗶𝗱𝗱𝗹𝗲 𝗘𝗮𝘀𝘁  

In February 2026, Saudi Arabia’s Oversight and Anti-Corruption Authority (Nazaha) announced arrests in multiple fraud cases totaling over SR 10 million (~$2.6M). These included a PIF-affiliated project manager accepting SR 2.2 million in bribes for contract awards and other public/private sector employees involved in bribery, embezzlement, and illegal document issuance ~ underscoring risks from unverified insider integrity.

With remote/hybrid hiring, global talent pools, and AI making sophisticated fakes easier, one-time checks are insufficient. Continuous, tech-enabled verification (education, employment history, criminal records, references) has become essential risk management.

HR leaders, CHROs, and business owners in India, GCC & East Asia: Make robust background screening your first line of defense. It protects assets, culture, and stakeholder trust ~ not just a compliance checkbox.

#BackgroundVerification #EmployeeFraud #HiringRisks #CorporateGovernance #IndiaHR #SaudiArabia #RiskManagement #AntiCorruption

**Most Kenyan SMEs lose money to vague contracts—here's how to fix it.**

A contract without clear payment terms is a contract waiting to fail. We've reviewed thousands of agreements across East Africa, and the pattern is stark: 67% of disputes stem from ambiguous clauses, not malice.

Here's what works:

1. **Define payment milestones in writing.** Don't rely on verbal agreements. Specify amounts, dates, and consequences for late payment. The CBK recognizes written evidence in commercial disputes.

2. **Use templates aligned to Kenyan law.** Whether you're hiring, partnering, or selling—your contract must reference the Employment Act 2007, Partnership Act, or relevant legislation. Generic templates create liability.

3. **Include dispute resolution clauses.** Add arbitration or mediation provisions before court. It's faster, cheaper, and keeps business relationships intact.

Your contracts are your safety net. SharkFlow Legal uses AI to generate Kenyan-compliant agreements in minutes—cutting legal costs by 70% while protecting your business.

Don't leave money on the table. Secure your contracts today.

Visit legal.sharkflowltd.com to get started.

High above the city at Salesforce Tower, CEO Joseph Orsborn, founder of Lilypad Learning (the first AI-native operating system for special education) shared how he went from Massachusetts Institute of Technology to founder

At Silicon Valley Fellowship, he walked us through how he built Lilypad.

He grew up in Australia working in medical devices at Abbott. When he came to MIT for his MBA, he met people talking about private equity and startups, things he had no exposure to before. 

Someone with more experience told him to follow a very interesting idea, improving mental health access for kids. That's when it clicked for him. He didn't see himself staying in medical devices, so he used his time at MIT to reflect and commit to building something in mental health.

The early days were hard. He talked to 20 different schools before getting his first yes, an $8,000 contract. He said it felt so exciting at the time. Now Lilypad Learning serves districts across the country and the numbers are way higher.

He highlighted something important about regulated industries like education and healthcare. Historically, you had to earn your way in with services before you could sell software, but AI is completely changing that game. Software is so easy to scale, and with AI you can optimize and create value faster than ever before.

He also talked about hiring. His first engineer was bad. The second one transformed the company. Choosing the right person can truly change an organization. That's something I think about a lot as I work on my projects, bringing that energy of change for the better, like I do in LEAD.

He adviced us to find what we are most passionate about, research all the competitors in that space (what they have in common, how they became successful, how they differentiate). 

He also said to talk to at least 100 people, be a good listener (that's the key to being a good salesperson), develop real relationships, and, don't be afraid of feedback, know how to react, know how to adjust.

Thank you Joe for sharing your journey and being so transparent. These lessons are so inspiring for me as I think about my own path in tech.

> Small Language Models (SLMs): A Small Business's Secret Weapon :

> You've probably heard a lot about AI lately ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini. These are Large Language Models (LLMs) and they're impressive. But they're also expensive, slow and honestly overkill for most small businesses. Enter Small Language Models (SLMs) the leaner, faster, more affordable cousins that are quietly becoming a game-changer for small business owners.

> So What Even Is an SLM?

> Think of a large language model like hiring a Harvard-educated consultant who knows everything about everything. An SLM is more like hiring a sharp specialist who knows your industry really well. It's a compact AI model trained to do specific tasks and it does them really, really well without needing a supercomputer to run.

> Why Should a Small Business Care?

> Running a large AI model is like keeping the lights on in a stadium. SLMs are more like a desk lamp they do the job without the electricity bill. Small businesses can run SLMs on affordable hardware or even on a laptop. No expensive cloud subscriptions, no per-query fees adding up every month.

> Because they're smaller, SLMs respond faster. For a customer-facing chatbot answering questions on your website, speed matters. Nobody wants to wait five seconds for an AI to think about a return policy.

> Here's something most people overlook. With a big cloud-based AI, your data customer info, business documents, internal emails leaves your building. With an SLM, you can run it locally on your own machine. Your data stays yours. For a small accounting firm, a medical practice or a legal office, this isn't just nice to have, it's essential.

> It Can Be Trained on Your Business
> SLM can be fine-tuned on your specific products, your tone of voice, your FAQ documents, your industry jargon. The result is an AI that actually sounds like you and knows your business not a generic bot giving generic answers.

> Real Examples of SLMs at Work:
> A bakery uses an SLM to handle customer order inquiries and custom cake requests via WhatsApp automatically, overnight, without staff.

> A small law firm uses one to draft first versions of standard contracts, saving hours of repetitive typing.

> A local e-commerce shop uses an SLM to auto-generate product descriptions from a simple list of features.

We’re Hiring: Python Geospatial (GIS) (Contract Position)

Experience: 5-7 Years
Location: Remote
Hiring Mode - Contract
Notice Period: Immediate joiners Only

Note : - Please Don't Apply if you are not open for Contract

Job Description:
Work with large-scale geospatial datasets and develop robust Python-based data processing pipelines.
Implement advanced spatial SQL queries and work with relational databases for geospatial analysis.
Develop and automate end‑to‑end workflows for the eNGTM Auto‑Matching processes.
Apply geospatial machine learning and AI techniques to improve accuracy and matching performance.
Utilize tools such as FME, ArcGIS, and QGIS to transform, validate, and analyze spatial data.
Build and maintain AWS-based architectures for scalable data processing and application deployment.
Design and implement API-driven data extraction, including handling JSON and XML formats.
Collaborate with engineering teams, data scientists, and customer stakeholders to refine requirements and deliver high‑quality solutions.
Contribute to coding standards, review peer code, and ensure high-quality, efficient, and reusable software.
Participate in agile development processes, knowledge sharing, and continuous improvement initiatives.
